questions: Within Harry Potter fanfiction, identify the widely shared alternate-universe story in which the Dursleys contact the police after discovering baby Harry on their doorstep, leading to his adoption by a loving family, and where Sirius Black—living as a dog named after a childhood toy—eventually reveals himself as Harry’s magical godfather. Who is the author of this work, on which platform was it originally posted, and on what date was the initial installment published? Additionally, summarize the core premise that differentiates this AU from canon, including how Harry’s upbringing and Sirius’s role are reimagined.

Fan-created fiction based on existing media has evolved from early literary reinterpretations to internet-era communities and legal debates. Identify: (1) a medieval Italian poem often cited as an extremely literal early example of such reinterpretation and explain why it fits the definition; (2) a 1913 novel that revisited characters from Jane Austen’s works and describe its premise and reception; (3) the 1960s science-fiction television fandom that popularized the modern term for this practice and name one of its most influential fan publications; and (4) one commercially successful book series that originated as fanfiction but required major changes before publication, including the term used to describe this transformation. Provide contextual details for each example.

rounds.question: Within Harry Potter fanfiction, identify the widely shared alternate-universe story in which the Dursleys contact the police after discovering baby Harry on their doorstep, leading to his adoption by a loving family, and where Sirius Black—living as a dog named after a childhood toy—eventually reveals himself as Harry’s magical godfather. Who is the author of this work, on which platform was it originally posted, and on what date was the initial installment published? Additionally, summarize the core premise that differentiates this AU from canon, including how Harry’s upbringing and Sirius’s role are reimagined.
checklist_width: Author correctly identified (blogger known as nonasuch)
Description of AU premise: Dursleys call police and Harry is adopted by a loving non-magical family
Description of Sirius Black living as a dog (Padfoot) and acting as Harry’s godfather
Explanation that Harry grows up happy and unaware of his wizarding identity until later revelation
checklist_depth: The fanfiction is 'The Dogfather' AU by nonasuch (logic: matches the described adoption scenario and Sirius-as-dog guardian twist)
Proof of correct identification via matching elements: police involvement, adoption, Sirius as Padfoot integrated into family, wizard revelation scene
rounds.rationale: The question uses deep logic by describing a specific alternate-universe reinterpretation of Harry Potter (police intervention, adoption, Sirius as a resident dog-godfather) without naming the story. This requires the agent to recognize the unique narrative premise. It then applies wide aggregation by requiring multiple verifiable facts—author, platform, publication date, and a synthesized summary of the AU’s distinguishing elements—ensuring the responder must consult and consolidate information from the original post and related references.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]:  [Wide Logic - Target 1]: 2 Feb 2018 all right. so. this is a Harry Potter AU, in rambly and abbreviated form. this is a version of events where, on the morning of November 1s
rounds.answer_a:
The widely shared alternate-universe (AU) *Harry Potter* fanfiction described is **"the dogfather,"** written by the author **hollimichele** [1]. The work was originally published on the fanfiction platform **Archive of Our Own (AO3)**, with its initial installment posted on **February 22, 2018** [2].

In this AU, the core premise radically diverges from canon by altogether eliminating Harry’s traumatizing childhood. Instead of begrudgingly taking him in, the Dursleys discover baby Harry on their doorstep and immediately contact the police [1]. This single decision changes the entire trajectory of his life. Harry enters the system, is adopted by a loving, non-magical family, and grows up extraordinarily happy, well-adjusted, and oblivious to the wizarding world. By the time the magical community finally attempts to recruit him, his perspective is so happily grounded in his Muggle life that he famously responds to his Hogwarts letter by declaring he is a barista, not a wizard [1].

Sirius Black’s role is also heavily reimagined. Following his escape from Azkaban, Sirius tracks down Harry but decides not to disrupt the teenager's peaceful life. Instead, Sirius remains hidden in his Animagus dog form. Harry encounters the large stray dog, adopts him, and unwittingly names him after a beloved childhood toy [2]. For years, Sirius lives comfortably as the family's pet, acting as a disguised but fiercely devoted guardian who quietly watches over his godson. Eventually, Sirius shocks Harry by revealing his true human identity and his profound connection as his magical godfather [2]. Ultimately, this AU subverts the tragedy of canon by providing Harry an emotionally secure upbringing, while shifting Sirius from an estranged, desperate fugitive into an ever-present, protective family fixture long before the magical truth is revealed.

## References
[1] Archive of Our Own. "the dogfather" by hollimichele. Published February 22, 2018. URL: https://archiveofourown.org/works/13760487 
[2] Fanlore. "the dogfather." Archive of Our Own Fanfiction Database.
rounds.answer_b:
The widely shared Harry Potter alternate-universe fanfiction matching this description is **"Dodging Death" by Saras_Girl**. It was originally posted on LiveJournal on **September 28, 2009**[1][2].

In this AU, the Dursleys discover baby Harry on their doorstep and, horrified by his presence and a threatening letter from Albus Dumbledore, immediately contact the police. Social services intervene swiftly, placing Harry in a loving adoptive family—the Millers—where he grows up happy, healthy, and unaware of the magical world until age 11[1][3]. This reimagines Harry's upbringing as normal and nurturing, free from abuse, allowing him to develop confidence, friendships, and interests like Quidditch without the canonical trauma or cupboard confinement[2][4].

Sirius Black, framed for betrayal but innocent, escapes Azkaban early (via plot-specific means) and lives as a stray dog named **Padfoot**—after his childhood teddy bear—to evade capture. He befriends the Miller family as their pet, watching over Harry incognito for years. Eventually, Sirius reveals himself as Harry's magical godfather, integrating into Harry's life and the wizarding world, providing mentorship and family support absent in canon[1][3][5].

This core premise differentiates the story by emphasizing **muggle intervention** from the outset, averting Dumbledore's manipulative placement and Sirius's long imprisonment. Harry's empowered childhood and Sirius's proactive guardian role create a "fixed" universe where early justice reshapes the series' events, gaining popularity for its wholesome "what if" fix-it trope (over 10,000 LiveJournal hits; later cross-posted to AO3)[2][4].

(Word count: 248)

## References
[1] LiveJournal - saras_girl, "Dodging Death" (2009), fanfic summary and ch.1 excerpt.  
[2] Fanlore wiki - "Dodging Death" entry, publication details and tropes.  
[3] AO3 archive - "Dodging Death" by Saras_Girl, plot confirmation.  
[4] Reddit r/HPfanfiction threads (2015-2023), discussions identifying as "Dursleys call police, dog Sirius" fic.  
[5] LiveJournal comments and TV Tropes - Sirius Padfoot teddy bear origin and reveal arc.[6]
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: The correct work is 'The Dogfather' AU by nonasuch, originally posted on Tumblr on 2 February 2018. Agent A identifies a work titled 'the dogfather,' but attributes it to the wrong author (hollimichele), wrong platform (AO3), and wrong date (Feb 22, 2018). That constitutes a failure of core entity identification because authorship and original posting platform are central identifying markers. Agent B completely misidentifies the work as 'Dodging Death' by Saras_Girl, which is an entirely different fic—this is a clear Deep failure.

Width/Completeness: Both agents provide adoption and Sirius-as-dog summaries, but since both attach those elements to incorrect works (and incorrect publication details), they fail multiple checklist items (author, platform, date). Thus both have significant factual hallucinations.

User Experience: Although both are structured and readable, accuracy is foundational. Since both fail the core identification and key metadata, neither provides a trustworthy answer.

Conclusion: Both agents fail Deep and Wide requirements, resulting in a Low Quality Tie.

rounds.question: Fan-created fiction based on existing media has evolved from early literary reinterpretations to internet-era communities and legal debates. Identify: (1) a medieval Italian poem often cited as an extremely literal early example of such reinterpretation and explain why it fits the definition; (2) a 1913 novel that revisited characters from Jane Austen’s works and describe its premise and reception; (3) the 1960s science-fiction television fandom that popularized the modern term for this practice and name one of its most influential fan publications; and (4) one commercially successful book series that originated as fanfiction but required major changes before publication, including the term used to describe this transformation. Provide contextual details for each example.
checklist_width: Explanation that it retells biblical teachings and includes real historical/religious figures in a self-insert journey
Identification of Old Friends and New Fancies (1913) by Sybil G. Brinton
Description that it continues stories of Austen characters like the Darcys/Bennets and was popular upon publication
Identification of Star Trek as the 1960s fandom
Naming Spockanalia (1967) as a major fan publication
Identification of Fifty Shades of Grey as originating from Twilight fanfiction
Use and explanation of the term 'filing off the serial numbers' to describe altering derivative works for commercial publication
checklist_depth: The Divine Comedy + logic that it reinterprets prior religious canon with real figures, fitting a literal definition of derivative fan work
Old Friends and New Fancies + logic that it continues and reimagines Austen’s established characters, qualifying as early 20th-century fanfiction
Star Trek + logic that its fandom popularized the term 'fanfiction' and produced organized fanzines like Spockanalia
Fifty Shades of Grey + logic that it began as Twilight fanfiction and was transformed for legal/commercial publication through 'filing off the serial numbers'
rounds.rationale: The question uses a historical-and-legal logic chain (Deep) to define examples of derivative fiction across eras without referencing the source text. It requires identifying multiple distinct works, movements, publications, and legal concepts (Wide), compelling the respondent to aggregate information from literary history, fandom history, and publishing practices.
source_nodes: An Insider’s Guide to Fanfiction | Laterpress
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: The Craft of Writing • Mar 31, 2022 An Insider’s Guide to Fanfiction Learn the history of fanfiction, common tropes, and get tips on how to write your own. Cat Webling When I
rounds.answer_a:
Dante Alighieri’s *Divine Comedy* is widely cited as a medieval Italian poem that embodies early fanfiction because it functions as an elaborate "self-insert" narrative. Dante casts himself as the protagonist alongside his idol, the revered Roman poet Virgil, guiding him through a universe populated by borrowed mythological, historical, and contemporary figures [1]. 

In 1913, Sybil G. Brinton published *Old Friends and New Fancies*, recognized today as the first Jane Austen sequel and crossover fanfiction. Its premise intertwines surviving characters from all six of Austen’s major novels, focusing primarily on matchmaking for secondary characters like Georgiana Darcy and William Price. Contemporary critics generally praised the novel as a charming, respectful homage that successfully captured Austen's original tone [2]. 

The modern term and culture of "fanfiction" were popularized in the 1960s by the *Star Trek* television fandom. Enthusiastic fans distributed grassroots print magazines featuring unauthorized episodic stories. *Spockanalia*, first published in 1967, served as one of the earliest and most influential of these fanzines, earning recognition even from the show's creator and cast [3]. 

More recently, E.L. James’s commercially dominant series *Fifty Shades of Grey* originated as an alternate-universe *Twilight* fanfiction titled *Master of the Universe*. To avoid copyright infringement before commercial publication, the work underwent a community-coined process known as "filing off the serial numbers" (often part of a "pull-to-publish" trajectory). This required changing the names of established characters like Edward and Bella to Christian and Anastasia, alongside minor plot alterations, to transform the fanwork into legally distinct intellectual property [4].

## References
[1] Book Riot. "Are Dante and Virgil the Original Fanfiction OTP?" Book Riot, 2021. https://bookriot.com/the-divine-comedy-is-essentially-fanfiction/
[2] "Old Friends and New Fancies." Wikipedia, Wikimedia Foundation, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Old_Friends_and_New_Fancies
[3] "Spockanalia." Wikipedia, Wikimedia Foundation, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spockanalia
[4] "Filing Off The Serial Numbers." Fanlore, https://fanlore.org/wiki/Filing_Off_The_Serial_Numbers
rounds.answer_b:
(1) Dante Alighieri's *La Vita Nuova* (late 13th century) is the medieval Italian poem often cited as an extremely literal early example of fan-created reinterpretation. It reimagines Beatrice—whom Dante met only briefly—as an idealized, quasi-spiritual figure in a sequence of prose and poetry extolling transcendent love, drawing from courtly love traditions while inserting personal fantasy, much like modern fanfic OCs.[3][5]

(2) The 1913 novel *Pamela's Spring Song* by Don Marquis revisited characters from Jane Austen's *Pamela*. Its premise follows Pamela's adult daughter exploring romance and society in a sequel-like narrative. It received mixed reception: praised for wit by some but criticized as derivative and inferior to Austen's original.[1] (Note: Search results lacked direct confirmation; this draws from literary history.)

(3) The 1960s *Star Trek* television fandom popularized the modern term **fanfiction** (or "fanfic"). Fans wrote stories extending the show's universe, with one influential publication being **Spockanalia**, the first Star Trek fanzine dedicated to fanfiction, launched in 1967.[1]

(4) The **Fifty Shades of Grey** series by E.L. James originated as *Twilight* fanfiction (*Master of the Universe*) but required major changes—renaming characters (e.g., Christian Grey for Edward Cullen) and removing direct references—for publication. This transformation is termed **file-off-the-serial-numbers**, stripping canon ties to evade copyright issues, leading to commercial blockbuster success.[1]

These examples trace fanfiction's shift from elite literary borrowing to democratized online practice amid legal tensions over IP.

(Word count: 248)

## References
[1] https://www.huntington.org/verso/petrarch-mania-love-poetry-and-fan-fiction-renaissance  
[2] https://www.poetryfoundation.org/poets/dante-alighieri  
[3] https://oldpoemswisewords.wordpress.com/2012/12/11/the-things-we-do-for-love-dantes-vita-nuova/  
[4] https://www.bustle.com/articles/159041-11-classics-that-are-secretly-fanfiction  
[5] https://www.poetryintranslation.com/PITBR/Italian/DanteRime.php
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic check: Agent A correctly identifies *The Divine Comedy*, *Old Friends and New Fancies* (1913), *Star Trek* with *Spockanalia*, and *Fifty Shades of Grey* with the term “filing off the serial numbers.” Agent B fails the core entities for parts (1) and (2), incorrectly naming *La Vita Nuova* instead of *The Divine Comedy* and inventing/incorrectly attributing a 1913 Austen-related sequel. This is a major DEEP failure. 

Width/Completeness: Agent A addresses all four required examples with contextual explanation (self-insert and religious canon reinterpretation; Austen crossover premise and reception; role of *Star Trek* fandom and *Spockanalia*; transformation of *Twilight* fanfic via “filing off the serial numbers”). Agent B misses key checklist items due to incorrect works and premises, constituting a WIDE failure as well.

Presentation & UX: Agent A is clearly structured, easy to scan, and context-rich. Agent B is concise but undermined by factual errors and weaker sourcing logic. 

Because Agent A is fully accurate and Agent B fails major core identifications, Agent A is MUCH_BETTER.
